bugs.title: setInterval handle stored in Zustand state is never cleared on unmount
bugs.description: The PuzzleGameStore stores a setInterval handle (timerInterval) in Zustand state. When the component using this store unmounts (e.g., user navigates away from the game mid-play), there is no mechanism to clear this interval. The interval continues running in the background, calling set() on the store and potentially triggering completeLevel(false) after the user has already left the game.
bugs.impact: Memory leak and potential state corruption. The interval fires indefinitely in background, updating store state for a game the user is no longer playing. Could cause unexpected UI flashes if the user returns to the game later.
bugs.suggested_fix: Do not store interval handles in Zustand state. Instead, manage the timer in a React useEffect hook within the PuzzleWorld component, which automatically cleans up on unmount. Alternatively, add a cleanup function that clears the interval when the component unmounts.

bugs.title: Timer uses stale timeRemaining value in setInterval closure
bugs.description: The useTimer hook creates a setInterval that captures timeRemaining from the render cycle. The useEffect depends on timeRemaining, so every tick causes a re-render, clears the old interval, and creates a new one. This means the interval is being recreated every single second, which is wasteful and fragile. More critically, if the effect cleanup races with the interval callback, timeRemaining could be stale, causing double-decrements or skipped values.
bugs.impact: Timer may tick inconsistently, potentially counting down faster or slower than expected. The constant interval creation/destruction adds unnecessary overhead.
bugs.suggested_fix: Use a ref to track timeRemaining inside the interval, and only use the effect to start/stop the interval based on gameState changes. Alternatively, use requestAnimationFrame with a timestamp comparison for accurate timing.

bugs.title: WordRain game loop uses stale wordRainWords in requestAnimationFrame
bugs.description: The game loop effect in WordRain captures wordRainWords from the render cycle and uses it inside a requestAnimationFrame callback. Because rAF callbacks run asynchronously, by the time the callback executes, the wordRainWords array may have already changed (e.g., a word was removed). The effect lists wordRainWords as a dependency, causing the rAF to be cancelled and recreated on every word position update, which defeats the purpose of using rAF for smooth animation.
bugs.impact: Janky word movement, potential duplicate word removal calls, and unnecessary rAF re-registrations on every frame. Words may visually stutter or jump.
bugs.suggested_fix: Use a ref to always access the latest wordRainWords without causing the effect to re-run. The rAF loop should only be started/stopped based on gameState, and it should read word data from a ref.

bugs.title: Timer tickTimer function uses integer countdown that drifts from real time
bugs.description: The tickTimer function decrements timeRemaining by 1 each call, and is invoked by a setInterval(1000). However, setInterval is not guaranteed to fire at exactly 1000ms intervals (it can drift due to browser throttling, tab backgrounding, or CPU load). Over a 30-second timer, the displayed countdown could drift several seconds from the actual elapsed time. This same pattern appears in geoStore.ts (line 452), wordBuilderStore.ts (line 568), scienceStore.ts, historyStore.ts, and animalStore.ts.
bugs.impact: Timer display becomes inaccurate, especially when the browser tab is in the background. A player could have more or less time than displayed.
bugs.suggested_fix: Track the actual deadline (Date.now() + timeLimit * 1000) and compute timeRemaining as Math.ceil((deadline - Date.now()) / 1000) on each tick. This ensures the countdown always reflects real wall-clock time.

bugs.title: Login accepts any credentials and navigates to dashboard
bugs.description: The handleSubmit function accepts any email/password combination and navigates to /dashboard after a 1-second setTimeout. There is no credential validation of any kind. The social login handler also bypasses any auth check entirely.
bugs.impact: Any visitor can access all authenticated pages (dashboard, games, shop, leaderboard) without any form of authentication. While the app has no real backend yet, this establishes a dangerous pattern.
bugs.suggested_fix: Implement a Zustand auth store that tracks login state. Create a ProtectedRoute wrapper that checks auth state and redirects to /login. At minimum, validate against stored credentials from signup.

bugs.title: Protected routes have no authentication guard
bugs.description: Routes for /dashboard, /games, /shop, and /leaderboard are marked as protected in comments but have no actual ProtectedRoute component or auth check. Any unauthenticated user can directly navigate to these URLs.
bugs.impact: All protected pages are publicly accessible. The mock user profile with hardcoded data is displayed to everyone.
bugs.suggested_fix: Create a ProtectedRoute component that wraps routes requiring authentication.
bugs.code_snippet:
{/* Protected routes (would need auth check in real app) */}
<Route path="/dashboard" element={<Dashboard />} />

bugs.title: Key comparison logic allows case-insensitive bypass in typing tutor
bugs.description: The handleKeyPress function uses key.toLowerCase() === expectedChar.toLowerCase() || (key === expectedChar) to determine correctness. The first condition makes the check case-insensitive (so 'a' matches 'A'), and the second is redundant since exact match is always a subset of case-insensitive match. For a typing tutor that should teach proper capitalization, this means pressing 'a' when 'A' is expected counts as correct.
bugs.impact: Players never learn proper capitalization. The accuracy metric is inflated because case errors are not penalized.
bugs.suggested_fix: For levels that teach capitalization (levels 3+), use strict comparison: const isCorrect = key === expectedChar;. For beginner levels (level 1-2), keep case-insensitive matching.

bugs.title: WordRain updates every word position via individual Zustand set calls per frame
bugs.description: On each animation frame, the game loop iterates through all words and calls updateWordRainWord(word.id, { y: newY }) individually, each of which triggers a Zustand set() call and a React re-render. With 5-10 words on screen, this creates 5-10 state updates per animation frame (60fps), resulting in 300-600 state updates per second.
bugs.impact: Severe performance degradation with noticeable frame drops, especially on lower-end devices. Each set() call triggers React re-renders of all subscribed components.
bugs.suggested_fix: Batch all word position updates into a single Zustand set() call. Create an updateAllWordPositions action that takes a map of id to newY and updates all words in one state transition. Or better, manage animated positions in a ref rather than in Zustand state.

bugs.title: Pattern display useEffect has too many dependencies causing re-triggers
bugs.description: The useEffect that controls pattern display timing depends on [gameState, currentLevel, pattern.length, currentPatternIndex, generatePattern, showNextPatternCell]. Since generatePattern and showNextPatternCell are Zustand actions that call set(), every state change in the store can cause this effect to re-fire, potentially creating multiple overlapping timeouts even though the ref-based cleanup tries to prevent it.
bugs.impact: Pattern cells may flash too quickly or skip steps during the showing phase if store updates happen between the timeout scheduling and firing.
bugs.suggested_fix: Use a stable ref for the Zustand actions and reduce the dependency array. Only depend on gameState and currentPatternIndex for the scheduling logic.

bugs.title: selectLevel uses setTimeout(0) to call initializeGrid which creates a race condition
bugs.description: When selectLevel is called, it sets the state to countdown and then uses setTimeout(() => get().initializeGrid(), 0) to initialize the grid. The setTimeout creates a window where the component renders in countdown state with no grid cells. If any component tries to access cells during this render, it will get an empty array.
bugs.impact: Brief flash of empty grid during countdown. If the countdown component tries to show a preview of the grid, it will display nothing.
bugs.suggested_fix: Call initializeGrid synchronously within the same set() call by computing all state at once: set({ currentLevel: level, gameState: 'countdown', cells: createGrid(level.gridSize), ... })

bugs.title: Misleading variable name maxWrongAllowed = 2 when 3 wrong answers trigger failure
bugs.description: The selectCell function uses const maxWrongAllowed = 2 and checks wrongSelections > maxWrongAllowed (i.e., fails at 3 wrong). The UI (MemoryMatrix.tsx line 405) displays wrongCount/3, which matches the actual behavior. But the variable name maxWrongAllowed = 2 is misleading since 3 wrong answers are actually what triggers failure.
bugs.impact: The variable name is misleading for maintainers. While the behavior happens to be correct, the code reads as if only 2 wrong answers should be allowed.
bugs.suggested_fix: Rename to const maxWrongBeforeFail = 3 and change condition to wrongSelections >= maxWrongBeforeFail to make the intent clearer.

bugs.title: All lazy-loaded game components are instantiated as JSX on every render
bugs.description: The gameComponents record is created inside the component function body on every render, meaning all 12 React.lazy components are instantiated as JSX elements on every render, even though only one is used. While React.lazy prevents the actual module from loading, the JSX creation still happens for all 12 entries.
bugs.impact: Minor performance waste on every render creating 11 unused JSX elements.
bugs.suggested_fix: Use a switch statement or a lookup that returns a component reference rather than a JSX element. For example: const GameComponent = gameComponentMap[gameId]; return <GameComponent />;

bugs.title: Loop expansion has no depth limit enabling infinite recursion via nested loops
bugs.description: The expandProgram function recursively expands nested loops. If a user creates deeply nested loops with high repeat counts (e.g., loop 10 > loop 10 > loop 10 > move_forward), this creates 10x10x10 = 1000 expanded commands. With higher nesting or larger repeat values, this grows exponentially and could freeze the browser.
bugs.impact: Browser tab freeze or crash if a child creates deeply nested loops with high repeat counts.
bugs.suggested_fix: Add a maximum expanded command count (e.g., 500) and abort expansion if exceeded. Also cap loop repeat values to a reasonable maximum (e.g., 10).

bugs.title: Button press only activates first door in level ignoring linked door IDs
bugs.description: When the robot steps on a button tile or interacts with one, the code finds the door using tiles.find(t => t.type === 'door'), which always returns the first door. In levels with multiple doors and buttons, all buttons would only toggle the first door found in the tile array.
bugs.impact: Any level with multiple doors/buttons would not work correctly. Only the first door would ever be activated regardless of which button is pressed.
bugs.suggested_fix: Add a linkedId field to button tiles that references the specific door they should activate. Then find the door by ID: tiles.find(t => t.id === tile.linkedId)

bugs.title: nextProblem resets timeRemaining to full timeLimit giving extra time per question
bugs.description: The nextProblem function resets timeRemaining to currentLevel?.timeLimit on every problem transition. If timeLimit represents the per-question time limit, this is correct. But if it represents the round time limit, this gives players a full timer reset on every question, effectively giving them unlimited time.
bugs.impact: If timeLimit is meant to be a round timer, players get unlimited time because it resets per question. The timer becomes meaningless.
bugs.suggested_fix: Clarify whether timeLimit is per-question or per-round. If per-round, track the deadline separately and do not reset on nextProblem. If per-question, the current behavior is correct but should be documented.

bugs.title: Explore mode accuracy calculation can produce NaN via division by zero
bugs.description: In the completeGame function for explore mode, accuracy is calculated as (discoveredFacts.length / (visitedPlanets.length * 4)) * 100. If no planets have been visited (visitedPlanets.length === 0), this results in a division by zero, producing NaN or Infinity.
bugs.impact: If a player triggers completeGame without visiting any planets, the accuracy becomes NaN, which propagates to the star calculation and score, corrupting the results display.
bugs.suggested_fix: Add a guard: const accuracy = visitedPlanets.length > 0 ? (discoveredFacts.length / (visitedPlanets.length * 4)) * 100 : 0;

bugs.title: Timer interval can fire after game ends during state transition window
bugs.description: The timer useEffect creates an interval when gameState is playing. The cleanup runs when the effect re-runs. If tickTimer causes skipProblem which calls finishRound, the gameState transitions to results. However, the tickTimer function continues to be called from the still-active interval until React processes the effect cleanup.
bugs.impact: There is a brief window where tickTimer may fire after the game has ended, potentially calling skipProblem on an already-completed round.
bugs.suggested_fix: Add an early return in tickTimer if the current game state is not playing, or clear the interval immediately in finishRound.

bugs.title: Puzzle accuracy metric measures hint avoidance not solving accuracy
bugs.description: In completeLevel, accuracy is calculated as Math.min(100, Math.round((1 - hintsUsed / (currentLevel.hints || 1)) * 100)). This measures how few hints were used, not how accurately the puzzle was solved. A player who makes many wrong moves but uses no hints gets 100% accuracy.
bugs.impact: The accuracy metric is misleading. It does not reflect puzzle-solving skill, only hint avoidance.
bugs.suggested_fix: Rename the field to hintEfficiency or compute accuracy based on optimal moves vs actual moves: Math.min(100, Math.round((optimalMoves / Math.max(1, moves)) * 100))
bugs.code_snippet: const accuracy = completed ? Math.min(100, Math.round((1 - hintsUsed / (currentLevel.hints || 1)) * 100)) : 0;

bugs.title: Landing page claims 8 games but project has 12 implemented games
bugs.description: The landing page stat counter shows 8 Games and the section header says 8 games at launch. More coming soon. However, the project now has 12 implemented games. The games array on the landing page only contains 8 entries, missing the 6 newer games.
bugs.impact: Users see outdated information that understates the product offering.
bugs.suggested_fix: Update the stat to 12 or 14 (including unimplemented). Update the games array to include all implemented games. Update the section header.

bugs.title: Dashboard games array missing 6 implemented games
bugs.description: The Dashboard page has a hardcoded games array with only 8 entries. It is missing Space Exploration, Geography Explorer, Science Explorer, History Heroes, Animal Kingdom, and Puzzle World. Players using the Dashboard cannot see or access these newer games.
bugs.impact: 6 fully implemented games are not accessible from the Dashboard. Players must go to the full Games page to find them.
bugs.suggested_fix: Add the missing 6 games to the Dashboard games array, matching the data in GamesPage.tsx.

bugs.title: GameCard progress uses Math.random() causing inconsistent renders
bugs.description: In the Games section of the landing page, each GameCard receives progress={Math.floor(Math.random() * 100)}. Since Math.random() produces a new value on every render, this causes unnecessary re-renders and visual flicker of the progress bars. In StrictMode, this will produce different values on each render pass.
bugs.impact: Progress bars show different random values on every render, causing visual jitter.
bugs.suggested_fix: Use useMemo or a constant array of pre-computed random values: const mockProgress = useMemo(() => games.map(() => Math.floor(Math.random() * 100)), []);

bugs.title: No error boundary around lazy-loaded game components
bugs.description: React.lazy game components are wrapped in Suspense but not in an Error Boundary. If a lazy-loaded chunk fails to load (network error, corrupt bundle), the entire app will crash with an unhandled promise rejection instead of showing a user-friendly error.
bugs.impact: Network failures during game loading crash the entire app. The user sees a white screen with no way to recover.
bugs.suggested_fix: Wrap the Suspense component in a React Error Boundary that shows a 'Game failed to load, try again' message with a retry button.

bugs.title: Forgot password link navigates to non-existent route
bugs.description: The Forgot password? link points to /forgot-password, but this route is not defined in App.tsx. The catch-all route silently redirects the user to the landing page. Similarly, the Terms and Privacy links in SignupPage.tsx (lines 275-277) link to non-existent /terms and /privacy routes.
bugs.impact: Users trying to recover their password or view legal pages are silently redirected to the landing page with no error message.
bugs.suggested_fix: Either implement these pages, or change the links to buttons that show a Coming soon toast/modal.

bugs.title: Gravity force overwrites acceleration instead of accumulating with other forces
bugs.description: In the physics engine update loop, gravity is applied as obj.acceleration.y = gravity * ppm, which directly sets the Y acceleration. This overwrites any previously accumulated Y acceleration from other forces applied via applyForce().
bugs.impact: External forces applied to physics objects have their Y component ignored because gravity immediately overwrites acceleration.y. Upward forces from ramps, explosions, etc. would not work correctly.
bugs.suggested_fix: Change to obj.acceleration.y += gravity * ppm to accumulate gravity with other forces, or restructure the force application order.

bugs.title: Game metadata differs between LandingPage Dashboard and GamesPage
bugs.description: The same games have different difficulty, ageRange, and metadata across the three pages. For example, Typing Master is medium difficulty on LandingPage but easy on Dashboard and GamesPage. Physics Lab is hard on LandingPage but medium elsewhere. Mouse Expert has 'Ages 3-10' on LandingPage but '3-10' on Dashboard.
bugs.impact: Users see inconsistent game information depending on which page they view.
bugs.suggested_fix: Extract game metadata into a shared data file (e.g., src/data/games.ts) and import it in all three pages to ensure a single source of truth.
bugs.code_snippet:
// LandingPage: difficulty: 'medium' for typing-master
// Dashboard:   difficulty: 'easy' for typing-master

bugs.title: FooterColumn renders identical markup for both hash links and route links
bugs.description: The FooterColumn component has an if/else for link.href.startsWith('#') that renders the exact same <a> tag in both branches. The route links (like /dashboard) should use React Router's Link component instead of <a> tags to enable client-side navigation.
bugs.impact: Clicking route links in the footer causes a full page reload instead of client-side navigation. The if/else branching serves no purpose since both branches are identical.
bugs.suggested_fix: Use React Router's <Link to={link.href}> for non-hash links and <a href={link.href}> only for hash links. Remove the duplicate branch.

bugs.title: Unsafe 'as any' type assertion on characters prop
bugs.description: The TextDisplay component receives characters={characters as any}, bypassing TypeScript's type checking. This hides potential type mismatches between the Character type from the store and what TextDisplay expects.
bugs.impact: Type errors in the characters data structure would not be caught at compile time. Runtime errors could occur if the types drift apart during refactoring.
bugs.suggested_fix: Import the Character type and properly type the characters prop in TypingGameProps, or fix the type mismatch that necessitated the as any cast.

bugs.title: All 12 Zustand persist stores lack data migration and schema validation
bugs.description: None of the persisted Zustand stores include a version number or migration strategy. If the data shape changes in an update, old localStorage data could cause runtime errors. Affected stores: typing-master-progress, math-basics-progress, memory-matrix-progress, code-quest-progress, physics-lab-progress, space-exploration-progress, geography-explorer-progress, science-explorer-progress, history-heroes-progress, animal-kingdom-progress, puzzle-world-progress, word-builder-progress.
bugs.impact: After a code update that changes the persisted state shape, users with old localStorage data may experience crashes or data loss.
bugs.suggested_fix: Add a version number and migrate function to each persist config: persist((set, get) => ({...}), { name: 'store-name', version: 1, migrate: (state, version) => { ... } })
bugs.code_snippet:
persist(
  (set, get) => ({...}),
  { name: 'typing-master-progress' }  // no version or migrate
)

bugs.title: Password field only enforces minLength with no complexity requirements
bugs.description: The signup form password field only validates minLength={8}. There are no requirements for uppercase letters, lowercase letters, numbers, or special characters.
bugs.impact: Users can create extremely weak passwords like 12345678 or aaaaaaaa.
bugs.suggested_fix: Add client-side validation requiring at least one uppercase, one lowercase, and one number. Show a password strength indicator.

bugs.title: Story mode chapter and line indices are never updated making story unplayable
bugs.description: The storyChapterIndex and storyLineIndex are initialized with useState(0) but have no setter destructured and are never updated. In story mode, the user will always see the first line of the first chapter, with no way to advance.
bugs.impact: Story mode is effectively broken - it always shows the same text content. Users cannot progress through story chapters.
bugs.suggested_fix: Destructure the setters and implement chapter/line advancement logic on game completion.

bugs.title: Leaderboard list uses entry.rank as key which could collide across tab changes
bugs.description: The leaderboard list renders entries with key={entry.rank}. When switching between global, weekly, and family leaderboards, entries with the same rank number from different lists will not be properly re-rendered because React sees the same key and reuses the DOM node.
bugs.impact: When switching leaderboard tabs, entries may not animate properly or may briefly show stale data from the previous tab's same-ranked entry.
bugs.suggested_fix: Use a composite key that includes the leaderboard type: key={`${leaderboardType}-${entry.rank}`}

bugs.title: Mini keyboard keys use Math.random() in animate prop causing constant re-animation
bugs.description: In the MainMenu keyboard animation, each mini key uses Math.random() > 0.7 inside the animate prop to determine its background color animation. Since Math.random() produces a new value on every render, and Framer Motion re-evaluates animate on each render, the keys randomly start and stop their glow animations on every React re-render.
bugs.impact: Keyboard animation is chaotic and inconsistent. Performance waste from constant Framer Motion re-calculations.
bugs.suggested_fix: Pre-compute which keys should glow using useMemo, and pass stable animation values.
